Unknown.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems** through evidence-based innovation and cross-sectoral data integration. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Evidence for Resilient Agriculture (ERA) initiative** synthesized 2,916 studies across Africa to evaluate agronomic technologies for climate adaptation, soil fertility, and sustainable intensification (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/C3YBNN).
- **CIAT and partners** profiled Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) adoption in 34 countries, quantifying barriers, policy gaps, and resilience scores across production systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FO42EP).
Unknown.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Evidence for Resilient Agriculture (ERA) dataset** compiled 112,859 observations from 2,916 studies (1934–2024) to assess climate-resilient agronomy, soil fertility, and intercropping efficacy across Africa, enabling context-specific policy design (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/C3YBNN).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Services program** linked Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) adoption to climate knowledge transfer, supporting farmer decision-making and resilience-building under Flagship 1 (CCAFS) and Flagship 4 (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/117328).

• **Bioversity International** modeled climate change impacts on 75 priority crop wild relatives (CWR) in the SADC region using RCP 4.5/8.5 scenarios, mapping species richness shifts to inform ex-situ conservation strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7ONUBJ).

• **CIAT’s CSA Country Profiles** quantified adoption levels, barriers, and policy gaps for CSA practices in 34 countries, engaging 1,500+ experts via participatory methods (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FO42EP).
Unknown.mitigation:
• **CGIAR/IFPRI** developed a global GHG emissions database for agriculture, disaggregated by country, commodity, and source (CH₄, CO₂, N₂O), using IPCC Tier 1 methodology to align with FAOSTAT emissions (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/81RZBS).

• **CGIAR’s DSSAT-based projections** modeled yield changes (2005–2050) for 7 crops under SSP370/SSP585 scenarios, isolating CO₂ fertilization effects and climate-model variability (ISIMIP3b/CMIP6) (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MXJDUC).
Unknown.water:
• **CGIAR’s HarvestChoice initiative** generated a global 5-arc-minute soil profile database, deriving hydraulic properties (e.g., saturated conductivity, wilting point) critical for crop modeling, leveraging SoilGrids and AfSIS data (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/1PEEY0).

• **CGIAR’s regional precipitation dataset** provided dekadal rainfall averages (2003–2012) for Cagayan Province, supporting localized water management applications (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MXJJ7U).

Kenya.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive capacity** in **Kenya** through evidence-based interventions, policy integration, and climate-smart agricultural practices. Specifically:
- **CIAT** evaluated climate-smart soil practices to enhance soil fertility, reduce erosion, and stabilize crop/livestock production in Western Kenya (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/K6JQXC).
- **CCAFS** integrated climate adaptation into national policies, aligning Kenya’s frameworks with global goals like the **Paris Agreement** and **SDGs** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110582).
Kenya.adaptation:
• **CIAT** conducted a **cost-benefit analysis of climate-smart soil practices** (e.g., agroforestry, organic manure) in Western Kenya to improve soil nitrogen cycles, reduce erosion, and enhance farmer resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/K6JQXC).

• **CCAFS** developed **Kenya County Climate Risk Profiles** (2016–2021) to assess climate vulnerabilities, prioritize value chains (e.g., food security, poverty reduction), and inform adaptation strategies across 45 rural counties (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MCGKE4).

• **CCAFS** evaluated **index-based insurance-linked credit products** (e.g., Risk Contingent Credit) in Machakos County, demonstrating their role in improving food security and coping mechanisms during droughts (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/OBZUCX).

• **CCAFS** analyzed **policy frameworks** linking climate adaptation to agriculture/food security, highlighting institutional gaps and co-benefits of mitigation-adaptation strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110582).

• **CCAFS** characterized **aquaculture livelihoods** in Kenya, linking climate factors to production practices and food security (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EJF326).

• **CCAFS** explored **digital tools for fisheries management** in coastal regions, assessing climate vulnerability and weather forecast adoption among fisherfolk (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WOL3MR).
Kenya.water:
• **CIAT** assessed **climate-smart soil practices** with water-related co-benefits, including reduced erosion and improved soil moisture retention in Western Kenya (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/K6JQXC).

• **CCAFS** generated **forage suitability maps** for Kenya using GIS tools to match climate/soil criteria (e.g., precipitation, elevation) with forage varieties (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FNEGDP).

Colombia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, sustainable land use, and resilience-building** in **Colombia** through **gender-disaggregated vulnerability assessments, climate-resilient crop breeding, and eco-efficient livestock systems**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** advanced **climate-resilient germplasm** for common beans under drought (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JR4X4C).
- **CCAFS** conducted **gender-disaggregated climate vulnerability surveys** to inform targeted adaptation strategies (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/28324).
- **Bioversity/CIAT** explored **sustainable beef production** with reduced environmental impact (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FMGTQF, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/CJQWGU).
Colombia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** assessed **climate vulnerability and adaptive capacity** in Bogotá’s smallholder livelihoods, linking exposure to crop impacts and rural resilience frameworks (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HA22TC).
• **CIAT** mapped **agronomic traits for drought tolerance** in common beans via genetic mapping, enabling climate-resilient breeding (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JR4X4C).
• **CCAFS** conducted **gender-disaggregated surveys** on risk perception and Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) adoption, revealing differential vulnerabilities (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/28324).
• **CIAT** analyzed **climate risks in key value chains** (cocoa, maize, beef) and proposed **adaptation measures** for supply chain resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/G9ADYV).
• **CIAT** investigated **yield variation factors** in maize (Córdoba) to inform climate-adaptive agronomic practices (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/LSDQ5C).
• **CGIAR** evaluated **climate-anthropic effects on páramo ecosystems** (e.g., Espeletia complex), addressing water and resilience in Andean highlands (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/GJZAIF).
Colombia.mitigation:
• **Bioversity/CIAT** surveyed **sustainable cacao agroforestry systems** in Caquetá/Cesar to quantify **carbon mitigation potential** and peacebuilding synergies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/G76RMO).
• **CIAT** researched **consumer preferences for low-carbon beef**, segmenting markets for eco-efficient livestock products (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FMGTQF, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/CJQWGU).
Colombia.water: • **CIAT** generated **high-resolution climate datasets** (precipitation, evapotranspiration) for the **Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta**, supporting water management and hydrological modeling (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/YNNXO1).

India.narrative:
CGIAR’s **TAFSSA (Transforming Agrifood Systems in South Asia)** initiative prioritized **climate-resilient agrifood systems in India** through gender-inclusive, district-level assessments. Specifically:
- TAFSSA conducted **multi-stakeholder household and community surveys** in **Nalanda District** to evaluate **climate risk responses, natural resource management, and livelihood resilience** (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/V5FMHZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WQBKVH).
India.adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **TAFSSA** assessed **climate risk responses, agricultural adaptation strategies, and resilience-building practices** among male and female farmers in **Nalanda District**, focusing on **crop production, livestock, fish cultivation, and future farming aspirations** (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/V5FMHZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WQBKVH).
• The initiative documented **household-level climate adaptation measures**, including **asset ownership, social safety net participation, and migration patterns**, to inform policy and program design (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WQBKVH).
India.water:
• CGIAR’s **TAFSSA** evaluated **water access and natural resource management** as critical components of climate resilience, analyzing **household water availability, gender roles in water management, and integration with agricultural activities** in **Nalanda District** (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/V5FMHZ).
• The assessments linked **water access to food security, dietary patterns, and climate risk responses**, emphasizing **gender-disaggregated data** to address disparities in rural water governance (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/V5FMHZ).

Ethiopia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive agriculture** in **Ethiopia** through evidence-based interventions, policy integration, and participatory research. Specifically:
- **IFPRI** conducted rigorous impact evaluations of multisectoral graduation models to strengthen livelihoods, food security, and climate resilience under the **Productive Safety Net Programme (PSNP4)** (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NYTIMZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QDOZPM, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MBRDZ7).
- **CIMMYT-CCAFS** collaborated with national stakeholders to develop Ethiopia’s **National Climate Change Adaptation Strategy** and built local research capacity (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65079).
- **CCAFS** simulated future climate risks for key crops (e.g., maize) to inform adaptive strategies (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THC08J).
- **CIP** facilitated farmer-led **citizen science trials** (e.g., *tricot* method) to evaluate climate-adaptive crop varieties (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).
Ethiopia.adaptation:
• **IFPRI** evaluated the causal impacts of **multisectoral graduation packages** (livelihoods, nutrition, gender, and climate resilience) on PSNP4 households across **192 kebeles** in Amhara and Oromia regions, using clustered RCT designs to measure improvements in food security, child nutrition, and women’s empowerment (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NYTIMZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QDOZPM, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MBRDZ7).

• **CIMMYT-CCAFS** supported Ethiopia’s **National Climate Change Adaptation Strategy** by generating nationally relevant climate research and strengthening institutional capacity (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65079).

• **CCAFS** synthesized **local adaptation practices** across nine districts, documenting drought tolerance strategies and institutional responses to climate vulnerability (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/56997).

• **CCAFS** produced **high-resolution climate projections** (50-year simulations) to assess risks to maize yields and inform adaptive agricultural strategies (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THC08J).

• **CIP** enabled **farmer participation in on-farm research** via *tricot* trials, testing **102 crop varieties** (beans, wheat) for climate resilience in Ethiopia (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).

• **CCAFS** reviewed Ethiopia’s **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) policies**, highlighting gaps in productivity and resilience for smallholder farmers using traditional technologies (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108473).

• **CCAFS** monitored **socioeconomic impacts of CSA practices** in **Doyogena and Basona Worena districts**, linking productivity gains to enhanced resilience (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).
Ethiopia.mitigation: • **CCAFS** demonstrated that **climate-smart agriculture (CSA)** in Ethiopia integrates **adaptation (resilience) and mitigation (reduced emissions per output)**, though evidence on emission reductions remains preliminary (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).

Uganda.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation** in **Uganda** through policy analysis, climate-smart agricultural (CSA) technologies, and vulnerability assessments. Specifically:
- **CIAT and IITA** analyzed gender-inclusive policy frameworks to strengthen grassroots climate adaptation (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67156**).
- **CCAFS** evaluated CSA adoption impacts on nutrition, resilience, and livelihoods in Northern Uganda (Sources: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HPMKAW**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/0ZEXKC**).
- **CGIAR** harmonized weather variability data to assess household welfare and agroecological vulnerabilities (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WP4PFG**).
- **CGIAR** conducted climate risk vulnerability assessments to inform sub-national decision-making (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/O8GOHP**).
- **CGIAR** developed context-specific CSA tools (e.g., CSA-RA) to prioritize gender-disaggregated adaptation strategies (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HCFDU8**).
Uganda.adaptation:
• **CIAT and IITA** evaluated institutional and political frameworks to integrate gender approaches into climate change policies, enhancing grassroots adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67156).
• **CCAFS** linked CSA technology adoption to improved nutrition, resilience, and food security in Northern Uganda (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HPMKAW).
• **CCAFS** conducted intra-household surveys to assess farm production resilience and CSA adoption in Uganda (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/0ZEXKC).
• **CGIAR** analyzed weather variability impacts on household welfare using agroecological zones (AEZ) and biophysical data (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WP4PFG).
• **CGIAR** assessed climate risk vulnerability (sensitivity, exposure, adaptive capacity) to guide sub-national climate adaptation strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/O8GOHP).
• **CGIAR** developed the **Climate Smart Agriculture Rapid Appraisal (CSA-RA)** tool to prioritize context-specific, gender-disaggregated adaptation solutions (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HCFDU8).
• **CGIAR** examined knowledge gaps between farmers and scientists in climate-adapted coffee/cocoa systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VQUR3W).
• **CGIAR** compiled data on climate hazards, vulnerability, and adaptive responses for African Green Climate Fund projects, including Uganda (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ).
Uganda.mitigation: • **CGIAR** explored climate-smart agricultural technologies (e.g., drought-resistant crops) as dual-purpose solutions for adaptation *and* mitigation in vulnerable farming systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VQUR3W).

Bangladesh.narrative:
CGIAR’s **TAFSSA (Transforming Agrifood Systems in South Asia)** initiative prioritized **climate-resilient agrifood systems in Bangladesh** through district-level, gender-integrated assessments. Specifically:
- TAFSSA conducted **multi-stakeholder surveys** (household, adolescent, market/retail) to link **climate risk responses, natural resource management, and livelihood resilience** across Rangpur and Rajshahi districts (Sources: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZDZVIZ**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QZL8NH**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/S8LYJR**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BA5X3F**).
Bangladesh.adaptation:
• CGIAR’s TAFSSA **assessed climate risk responses** in Bangladesh by analyzing **agricultural adaptation strategies** (crop production, livestock, fish cultivation) and **household resilience** through male/female/adolescent interviews in Rangpur and Rajshahi (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZDZVIZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QZL8NH).
• The Center **mapped market-level climate resilience** via surveys of 50 villages in Rangpur/Rajshahi, documenting food sourcing, wastage, and protection dimensions under agrifood systems stress (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/S8LYJR).
Bangladesh.water: • CGIAR’s TAFSSA **evaluated water access and natural resource management** as cross-cutting themes in Bangladesh**, integrating findings on **household water access** (female/male perspectives) and **land-water linkages** in climate risk assessments (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZDZVIZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QZL8NH, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/S8LYJR).

Nepal.narrative:
CGIAR’s **TAFSSA (Transforming Agrifood Systems in South Asia)** initiative prioritized **climate-resilient agrifood systems in Nepal** through gender-inclusive, district-level assessments. Specifically:
- TAFSSA conducted **multi-stakeholder surveys** (household, adolescent, and community) to evaluate **climate risk responses, agricultural resilience, and natural resource management** in Surkhet and Banke districts (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THVTYH, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XZRK2F).
- The Center integrated **market and retail surveys** to analyze food system vulnerabilities, sourcing, and climate adaptation strategies in rural Nepal (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BA5X3F).
Nepal.adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **TAFSSA** assessed **climate risk responses** in Nepal by documenting household-level adaptation strategies (e.g., crop diversification, livestock management) and gender-specific resilience practices among male and female respondents in Surkhet and Banke districts (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L).

• The initiative evaluated **future farming aspirations** and **asset-based adaptation** (e.g., access to credit, social safety nets) to strengthen livelihood resilience in rural Nepal (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L).

• TAFSSA’s **adolescent surveys** explored climate risk perceptions and aspirations, linking generational resilience to agrifood system adaptation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XZRK2F).

• The Center’s **retail and market surveys** in Banke and Surkhet districts analyzed **food system resilience**, including climate-related disruptions to supply chains and vendor adaptation strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BA5X3F).
Nepal.water:
• CGIAR’s **TAFSSA** integrated **water access** as a cross-cutting theme in Nepal, assessing its role in household food security and climate risk responses through female-led interviews in Surkhet and Banke (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THVTYH).

• The initiative documented **natural resource management** (water, land) as a critical component of climate adaptation, with gender-disaggregated data on water-related vulnerabilities (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L).

Honduras.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture, water resource planning, and agroclimatic adaptation** in **Honduras** through data-driven tools, climate scenario modeling, and field-based monitoring. Specifically:
- **CIAT** generated high-resolution climate surfaces and future projections to inform policy (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QET5UQ*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/E3C1KB*).
- **CCAFS** deployed Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) monitoring frameworks in villages like Santa Rita to track adoption, food security, and gender-disaggregated impacts (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/OSNTKT*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/A9SMP1*).
- **CIAT/CCAFS** developed agroclimatic indices and flood detection systems to support water planning and drought adaptation (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JZTN8Z*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UKW0QT*).
Honduras.adaptation:
• **CIAT** developed **30-second gridded historical climate surfaces** (precipitation, temperature) for decision/policy analysis, supporting Honduras’ Third National Communication to the UNFCCC (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QET5UQ).
• **CIAT** generated **future climate change scenarios** (2030s–2080s) for RCP pathways, providing downscaled projections of precipitation, temperature, and solar radiation to guide long-term adaptation planning (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/E3C1KB).
• **CCAFS** created a **database of agroclimatic indices** (1981–2022) linking dry conditions, heatwaves, and ENSO events to agricultural seasons, enabling climate-variability adaptation strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JZTN8Z).
• **CCAFS** implemented the **Integrated Monitoring Framework for Climate-Smart Agriculture** in Santa Rita Village, tracking CSA adoption, food security, and climate vulnerability at household/farm levels (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/OSNTKT, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/A9SMP1).
• **CCAFS** conducted **household surveys** in Ocotepeque/Copán to assess coffee farmers’ resilience, food insecurity, and livelihoods under climate variation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z29QGA).
• **CCAFS** identified **high-impact climate change spots** for drybeans yield using DSSAT simulations, classifying hotspots and adaptation zones across Central America (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HEQQIH).
• **CCAFS** collected **panel data (2018–2021)** on CSA adoption, climate services, and gender dynamics in Climate-Smart Villages (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/OWML6C).
Honduras.water:
• **CIAT** developed the **AGRI tool** (AGua para RIego), combining soil, hydrology, and DEM data to map **irrigation water sources** at 30m resolution, including field capacity, wilting point, and curve numbers for West Honduras (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QVXA7U).
• **CIAT/USAID** created a **flood detection system** using remote sensing (2017–2022), classifying annual flood events to support water resource planning via the *‘Agua de Honduras’* platform (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UKW0QT).

Ghana.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and adaptation strategies** in **Ghana** through data-driven interventions, cost-benefit analyses, and climate-smart agricultural (CSA) frameworks. Specifically:
- The Center developed **opportunity crop simulations** to secure food systems under climate change (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZFVCU9).
- Researchers evaluated **cost-benefit trade-offs of CSA practices** in the Coastal Savannah Agroecological Zone (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Q6BO6Q).
Ghana.adaptation:
• CGIAR **simulated climate impacts on food security** using opportunity crops as adaptive strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZFVCU9).

• The Center **conducted cost-benefit analyses of 8 CSA practices** in the Coastal Savannah Agroecological Zone, quantifying productivity gains and cost shifts (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Q6BO6Q).

• Researchers **harmonized household survey data** to assess weather variability’s effects on rural welfare, linking agroecological zones (AEZ) to vulnerability (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WP4PFG).

Guatemala.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and digital innovation** in **Guatemala** through **data-driven adaptation, resilience-building, and subnational climate strategies**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** deployed **integrated monitoring frameworks** to track CSA adoption, gender-disaggregated impacts, and climate vulnerability at household and farm levels (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VTPO4U*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/3GICDI*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/73LCA6*).
- **CCAFS and partners** leveraged **digital technologies** (e.g., sensor networks, Geofarmer app) to improve crop productivity and sustainability in maize and bean systems (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DGLBH7*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9VSYIZ*).
- **CGIAR researchers** simulated **spatial yield impacts** of climate change to identify high-risk and adaptation hotspots for drybeans (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HEQQIH*).
- **CCAFS facilitated subnational climate strategies** in Guatemala, aligning CSA interventions with national policies to address **productivity, adaptation, and mitigation** while improving rural livelihoods (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121329*).

Mali.narrative:

- The Center adapted and validated **RiceAdvice technology** in Mali’s rice systems, demonstrating **0.7 t/ha yield gains** over conventional fertilizer recommendations (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FZCCQ9**).

Vietnam.narrative:
CGIAR’s centers prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, gender-inclusive adaptation, and low-emissions development** in Vietnam through targeted research, mapping, and capacity-building. Specifically:

- **IRRI and CCAFS** analyzed **gender disparities in climate perception and adaptation strategies** in the Mekong Delta (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76928, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123229, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1108/ijccsm-01-2017-0014).

• **CCAFS and IRRI** documented **gender differences in climate perception**, revealing that women perceive climatic stress but adopt fewer farm-based adaptation strategies than men (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76928, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123229, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1108/ijccsm-01-2017-0014).

general / cross-cutting.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Evidence for Resilient Agriculture (ERA)** compiled **2,916 studies** to assess climate-resilient agronomy, soil fertility, and sustainable intensification across Africa, supporting policy and program design (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/C3YBNN).

• **TAFSSA** conducted **gender-disaggregated, district-level assessments** in Bangladesh, India, and Nepal to evaluate **climate risk responses, agricultural adaptation, and resilience** across crop/livestock systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/V5FMHZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THVTYH, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZDZVIZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WQBKVH).

• **SPIR (Ethiopia)** used a **clustered RCT** to test **multisectoral graduation interventions**, measuring impacts on climate resilience, food security, and livelihoods (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NYTIMZ).

• **CGIAR researchers in Kenya** evaluated **index-based insurance-triggered credit** as a coping mechanism for drought shocks in semi-arid regions (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/OBZUCX).
general / cross-cutting.water:
• **TAFSSA** integrated **water access assessments** into district-level agrifood systems evaluations in Bangladesh, India, and Nepal, linking water management to climate risk and resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/V5FMHZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THVTYH, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZDZVIZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WQBKVH).

• **TAFSSA’s adolescent assessments** in Bangladesh included **water access** as a cross-cutting natural resource management indicator (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QZL8NH).

rice.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers prioritized climate-resilient rice systems through **adaptation**, **mitigation**, and **water-efficient innovations**. Specifically:
- **IRRI** led climate-smart solutions for rice in **Vietnam’s Mekong Delta** and **South Asia**, integrating drought/salinity mapping, nutrient management, and rodent control (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111168, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106131, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JWUEQL).
- **Global models** (e.g., DSSAT) projected yield impacts under CO₂ fertilization and extreme climate scenarios (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MXJDUC).
- **Water-saving tools** (e.g., MapAWD) were developed to assess **Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD)** suitability for GHG mitigation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DUMAKG).
rice.adaptation:
• **IRRI** mapped **drought and salinity risks** for rice in Vietnam’s Mekong Delta, updating regional climate-smart adaptation plans (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111168).
• **IRRI** analyzed **gender differences** in climate perception and adaptation strategies among rice farmers in Vietnam’s Mekong Delta (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76928).
• **Bioversity/CGIAR** developed **agroclimatic indicators** to characterize rice exposure to **heat, drought, and flooding** stresses in Africa and Asia (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/LI5D3A, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/8FB13P).
• **IRRI** conducted systematic reviews on **Site-Specific Nutrient Management (SSNM)** for rice in Africa/Asia, linking climatic conditions to yield and nitrogen efficiency (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/H23MVL).
• **IRRI** piloted **Ecologically Based Rodent Management (EBRM)** in rice systems, demonstrating yield gains (0.7 t/ha) via community-led campaigns in Vietnam/Philippines/Indonesia (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JWUEQL).
• **CGIAR** launched **Big Data 2 CSA**, using digital analytics to tailor climate-smart solutions for **tens of thousands of smallholder rice/wheat farmers** in South Asia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106131).
rice.mitigation:
• **CGIAR** modeled **GHG emissions scenarios** (SSP370/SSP585) and CO₂ fertilization effects on rice yields using DSSAT, aligned with ISIMIP3b/CMIP6 climate projections (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MXJDUC).
• **IRRI** developed **MapAWD**, a tool to evaluate **AWD water-saving techniques** for GHG mitigation potential in rice fields (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DUMAKG).
rice.water:
• **IRRI** assessed **Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD)** suitability via spatially explicit water-balance models, supporting water-saving strategies in rice (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DUMAKG).
• **IRRI/ICON** studied **shift from flooded to non-flooded rice systems**, linking water management to disease dynamics (e.g., sheath blight) and crop rotation impacts (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9SPT9N).

maize.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient maize systems** through **digital agriculture, climate-smart practices, and adaptive supply chains**. Specifically:
- **CIAT/CIP** deployed **sensor-based monitoring** in Guatemala to track soil-climate interactions affecting maize productivity (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DGLBH7).
- **CIAT** simulated climate risks for maize in Ethiopia, integrating high-resolution projections to inform adaptive strategies (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THC08J).
maize.adaptation:
• **CIAT/CIP** developed **digital plot sensors** in Guatemala to analyze crop development conditions, enabling precision agriculture for maize sustainability (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DGLBH7).

• **CIAT** generated **climate projections for Ethiopia**, simulating maize yield impacts under extreme weather to guide adaptive policies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THC08J).

• **CIAT** assessed **maize-water limitations** in Nicaragua, linking deforestation and climate trends to yield declines, while documenting government-led adaptation responses (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/29206).

• **CIAT** tested **climate-smart maize-legume systems** in Tanzania, measuring soil moisture, infiltration, and physiological traits under variable weather (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/M0XCES).

• **CIAT** evaluated **supply chain vulnerabilities** in Colombia, proposing adaptation measures for maize value chains amid climate risks (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/G9ADYV).

• **CIAT** demonstrated **conservation agriculture (CA) trials** in Zambia, comparing no-till/dibblestick systems with maize-legume rotations for drought resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/RSGLGB).

• **CIAT** analyzed **yield variation factors** in Colombia, integrating climate, soil, and agronomic data for maize resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/LSDQ5C).

• **CIAT** explored **ratooning and crop rotation** in Southern Africa, testing pigeonpea-maize systems for drought tolerance (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EQZR5C).
maize.mitigation: • **CIAT** studied **soil organic carbon dynamics** in Tanzania, linking cultivation practices to carbon sequestration for climate-smart maize interventions (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/1YZAQZ).
maize.water:
• **CIAT** quantified **maize’s water limitations** in Nicaragua, contrasting its sensitivity with beans due to longer seasonal duration (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/29206).

• **CIAT** measured **soil water infiltration** in Tanzania, assessing climate-smart practices’ impact on maize productivity under variable rainfall (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/M0XCES).

coffee and cocoa.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers—including CIAT and collaborators—advanced climate-resilient coffee and cocoa systems through **adaptation-focused innovations** in Mesoamerica, West Africa, and Latin America. Specifically:
- **CIAT** led **Species Distribution Modeling (SDM)** to assess climate suitability shifts for coffee, cocoa, and agroforestry trees in Mesoamerica, identifying vulnerability hotspots (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/0O1GW1).
- The Center modeled **global Arabica coffee suitability** under 21 GCMs, projecting regional production risks by the 2050s (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/29634).
coffee and cocoa.adaptation:
• **CIAT and partners** used **ensemble SDM** to evaluate climate suitability for coffee, cocoa, and 100 agroforestry trees in Mesoamerica, guiding agroforestry diversification as an adaptation strategy (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/0O1GW1).

• **CGIAR (CIAT-led)** projected **Arabica coffee suitability declines** across major producing regions by 2050, using 2194 location points and 21 GCMs (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/29634).

• **CGIAR** quantified **economic losses from inaction** in Ghana’s and Ivory Coast’s cocoa sectors, advocating for climate adaptation investments (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97167, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100126).

• **CGIAR** defined **three adaptation zones** for West African cocoa (intensification, diversification, or crop change) based on 2030s/2050s climate projections from 15–19 GCMs (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/1JMXC6).

• **CGIAR** mapped **future cocoa suitability** in Ghana (2040–2069) under RCP 6.0, supporting regional scaling of adaptation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UWE6DP).

• **CGIAR** identified **knowledge gaps** between farmers and scientists in Uganda’s Arabica coffee systems, linking pest/disease perceptions to altitude and vegetation structure (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VQUR3W).

• **CGIAR** surveyed **348 coffee households** in Honduras to assess resilience, food insecurity, and livelihoods under climate variation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z29QGA).

• **CIAT** predicted **climatic suitability shifts** for cocoa in Ghana/Ivory Coast using MaxEnt modeling (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/27779).

• **CGIAR** generated **500m gridded climate suitability surfaces** for coffee in Risaralda, Colombia, using 5 SDM methods (GBM, RF, MaxEnt, GLM, GAM) (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FEEHQX).

legumes.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers prioritized **legume resilience and adaptation** through participatory breeding, climate-smart agricultural (CSA) practices, and socio-economic vulnerability assessments. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed climate-resilient germplasm via genetic mapping of drought-tolerant common bean traits (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JR4X4C).
- **CIAT/CIP** implemented crowdsourced variety trials (e.g., *tricot* method) to evaluate farmer-preferred bean varieties under abiotic stress (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).
- **CIAT** assessed gendered constraints in CSA adoption among bean farmers in Tanzania (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/LSP40V).
legumes.adaptation:
• **CIAT** conducted **crowdsourced tricot trials** in Nicaragua, Ethiopia, and India to evaluate yield and performance of 10 common bean varieties under farmer-managed conditions, linking observations to environmental stress data (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).

• **CIAT** mapped **drought-resilient agronomic traits** (e.g., Days to Flowering, Yield, Zinc/Iron content) in a MAGIC population of common bean (*Phaseolus vulgaris*) to support climate-adaptive breeding (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JR4X4C).

• **CIAT/CIP** simulated **spatial yield changes** due to climate change in Central America, identifying hotspots and adaptation spots for drybean production (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HEQQIH).

• **CIAT** evaluated **climate vulnerability determinants** among bush bean farmers in Colombia, integrating socioeconomic and climate perception data (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/E5UYJE).

• **CIAT/PABRA** assessed **impact of improved bean varieties** on smallholder productivity and food security in Burundi, using DNA fingerprinting and farmer recall (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UMR2OC).

• **CIAT** characterized **rural households** in Central America for climate adaptation interventions, focusing on common bean cultivation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PA2BCY).

• **CIAT/CIRAD-ILCA** studied **adaptability of forage legumes** (e.g., *Stylosanthes*, *Mucuna*) to humid/sub-humid regions in West/Central Africa via RABAOC trials (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/AGGBVZ).

• **CIAT** analyzed **gendered access to climate-smart agricultural technologies** among bean farmers in Tanzania, highlighting constraints in adoption (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/LSP40V).

• **CIAT** documented **variety adoption trends** of improved beans in Sub-Saharan Africa, linking uptake to income growth and resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MOCXUN).
legumes.water: • **CIAT** tested **climate-smart agricultural practices** in maize-legume systems, measuring soil water infiltration, moisture, and temperature to assess productivity under variable weather (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/M0XCES).

livestock (general/beef).narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers advanced **sustainable livestock and beef systems** through **data-driven adaptation, mitigation, and consumer-market linkages**, with a focus on **low-emission forages, agroecological transitions, and GHG quantification** in Latin America and sub-Saharan Africa. Specifically:
- **CIAT** (International Center for Tropical Agriculture) led **low-emission forage development** in Colombia, refining national mitigation strategies via nutritional modeling (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106300).
- **AReNA** (Advancing Research on Nutrition and Agriculture) integrated **geo-referenced climate-agroecology datasets** to link livestock systems with adaptation and water-resource constraints (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/OQIPRW).
- **LivestockPlus** quantified **GHG emissions from urine/manure patches** and carbon stocks in Colombia, providing inputs for national emissions factors (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IVYMGI).
livestock (general/beef).adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s AReNA** constructed a **multi-country DHS-GIS dataset** linking livestock systems with climate (temperature, rainfall, soil) and agroecological variables to inform adaptive strategies in South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/OQIPRW).
• **CIAT** reviewed **digital tools for agroecological transitions** in Brazil’s beef sector, assessing their role in **climate-informed adaptation** and farmer co-creation of knowledge (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DXPZ8G).
livestock (general/beef).mitigation:
• **CIAT’s LivestockPlus** developed **low-emission forage options** in Colombia by modeling digestion/metabolism, enabling policy-driven reductions in enteric methane and manure-derived N₂O (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106300).
• **CIAT** quantified **nitrous oxide (N₂O) fluxes from urine/manure patches** across Latin America, validating emission factors for improved pastures (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BM0ILQ).
• **CIAT** compiled **GHG emission databases** (methane, N₂O, carbon stocks) from Colombian pastures to refine national emissions inventories (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IVYMGI).
• **CIAT** modeled **N₂O fluxes** using DNDC simulations for Colombian livestock systems, linking soil emissions to mitigation strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HZG9KO).
• **CIAT** mapped **land-use change emissions** in Colombia’s Orinoco Region, providing mitigation pathways via sustainable agriculture (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NMNIEF).
• **CIAT** assessed **consumer willingness to pay (WTP)** for **low-impact beef** (improved forages) in Colombia, segmenting markets for sustainable intensification (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FMGTQF, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/CJQWGU).

fisheries and aquaculture.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **resilience and innovation in fisheries and aquaculture** across Africa, Asia, and the Pacific through **gender-inclusive adaptation strategies, digital tools for climate-informed decision-making, and on-farm performance assessments**. Specifically:
- **WorldFish** led **gender-impact evaluations** to build resilience in aquatic food systems (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IQXBDI).
- **AABS and PROFISHBLUE** scaled **digital fisheries management systems** to assess climate risks and adaptation strategies (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WOL3MR, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7J29HD).
- **Fish for Livelihoods** conducted **baseline surveys** on climate perceptions and adaptive practices in Myanmar (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZINQUU).
fisheries and aquaculture.adaptation:
• **WorldFish** conducted a **Climate-Hotspots Gender Impact Assessment** in Zambia to evaluate interventions (2018–2022) that enhanced fish farmers’ resilience through scaled research and innovations (Aquatic Foods Initiative) (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IQXBDI).

• **AABS (Asia Africa Bluetech Superhighway)** implemented a **digital fisheries management baseline** in Kenya, assessing climate vulnerability, weather forecast use, and adaptation strategies among 2,456 fisherfolk and traders (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WOL3MR).

• **PROFISHBLUE (SADC)** developed a **climate-informed Digital Fisheries Information System (FIS)** in Zambia and Malawi, identifying climate risks and adaptation strategies for 537 fish farmers and artisanal fishers (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7J29HD).

• **Fish for Livelihoods** surveyed **climate perceptions and adaptation strategies** in Myanmar, linking livelihoods to fish production resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZINQUU).

• **WorldFish** assessed **aquaculture production practices** in Kenya, integrating climate factors into livelihood analyses for 5,315 farmers (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EJF326).

• **CGIAR (Bangladesh study)** replicated data on **climate information services (CIS) for aquaculture**, defining temperature/rainfall thresholds to support adaptive decision-making in climate-sensitive operations (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EQPK7F).
fisheries and aquaculture.water:
• **CGIAR (Fish for Livelihoods + IWMI)** updated a **GIS database** of 2,652 smallholder aquaculture ponds in Myanmar, integrating the **Soil and Water Assessment Tool (SWAT)** to model climate-change impacts on water availability (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/TV7J27).

• **CGIAR** documented **water governance** in Cambodia’s Mekong Delta and Tonle Sap Lake, analyzing community fisheries and irrigation schemes (Tasoung Irrigation Scheme) (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/C1Y84W).

roots and tubers.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers prioritized climate-resilient cassava systems through targeted breeding, pest risk mapping, and climate-adaptive cultivation strategies. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed climate-specific cassava germplasm for semi-arid and sub-humid tropics, integrating pest/disease resistance and yield traits (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/L1TFKE).
- **CIAT** mapped global and African cassava climate regions using temperature, seasonality, and dry-season metrics to guide adaptive cultivation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WFAMUM, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/27503).
- **CIAT** generated pest risk maps for *Phenacoccus manihoti* (Cassava Mealybug) to predict climatic suitability and inform regional adaptation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/27604).
roots and tubers.adaptation:
• **CIAT** developed climate-specific cassava germplasm for **semi-arid and sub-humid tropics**, tailoring selection criteria to ecosystem-specific pests/diseases while maintaining yield and dry matter traits (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/L1TFKE).
• **CIAT** mapped **global and African cassava climate regions** based on growing season temperature, dry-season months, and seasonality to optimize adaptive cultivation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WFAMUM, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/27503).
• **CIAT** created **pest risk maps** for *Phenacoccus manihoti* (Cassava Mealybug) using climatic suitability indices to guide regional adaptation strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/27604).
• **TransFarm Africa** (via CGIAR partnerships) advanced **control measures for African cassava virus diseases**, addressing disease stress as an adaptation priority (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75222).

wheat.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient wheat systems** through participatory breeding, yield-enhancing technologies, and water management innovations. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Crop Science Centers** (e.g., IWIN, ICRISAT) developed **farmer-led variety evaluation frameworks** (e.g., tricot trials) to assess climate-adaptive wheat varieties in Ethiopia, India, and Nicaragua (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).
- **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience Programs** (e.g., CCAFS) promoted **weather-resilient technologies** in India, achieving **50% yield increases** despite climate-induced stress (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109732).
wheat.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Crop Science Centers** (e.g., IWIN) facilitated **crowdsourced tricot trials** in Ethiopia (durum wheat), India (bread wheat), and Nicaragua (common bean) to evaluate climate-adaptive varieties via farmer observations, linking performance data to environmental variables (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience Programs** (e.g., CCAFS) demonstrated that **climate change could reduce wheat yields by 6–23% by 2050** in India but showed **50% yield gains** through resilient practices in Madhya Pradesh, Bihar, and Uttar Pradesh (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109732).

• **CGIAR’s Genebank and Breeding Programs** upscaled **climate-adaptive wheat varieties** in Ethiopia, Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania, with **4,000+ farmers** adopting elite lines to improve livelihoods and adaptive capacity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121262).
wheat.water: • **CGIAR’s International Water Management Institute (IWMI)** developed **vertical drains** in Bihar, India, to **minimize seasonal waterlogging** (affecting 10.5% of irrigated wheat areas) while recharging aquifers for dry-season irrigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74948).

livestock (general/small ruminants).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient livestock systems** and **low-emissions sustainable production** across diverse agroecologies through targeted research and policy support. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** enhanced low-emissions livestock development and carbon crediting frameworks (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121203**).
- **CGIAR researchers** analyzed climate adaptation strategies in smallholder livestock systems, including fodder innovation and land management (Sources: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SMBVYF**, **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75052**).
livestock (general/small ruminants).adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s PRHPS survey** documented agricultural water use and climate change impacts on rural livestock-dependent households in Pakistan, linking water scarcity to production constraints (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/T9GGYA).
• **CGIAR researchers** assessed producer knowledge of climate change and land management practices (e.g., improved forages, conservation) among cattle/dairy farmers in Colombia’s Cauca Department (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SMBVYF).
• **CGIAR analyzed** land-use shifts in Zimbabwe’s Mid Zambezi Valley, showing how declining cotton profitability drove expansion of livestock grazing, altering wildlife coexistence and human-wildlife conflicts (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VMVZRD).
• **CGIAR highlighted** a case study of Susila Devi (Bihar, India), where climate-adaptive fodder solutions increased milk production and fat content despite water scarcity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75052).
livestock (general/small ruminants).mitigation: • **CGIAR’s CCAFS** provided technical support to the Asian Development Bank and World Bank to scale low-emissions sustainable livestock production (USD 215M) and plant-based meat initiatives, while guiding carbon crediting and soil carbon monitoring for the USD 500M Kazakhstan Sustainable Livestock Program (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121203).

agroforestry.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **agroforestry for climate resilience and mitigation** through policy development, threat mapping, and landscape-scale research. Specifically:
- **ICRAF** led the **National Agroforestry Policy in India**, integrating climate adaptation and mitigation into agricultural landscapes (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/35592).
- **ICRAF** mapped **multi-threat vulnerabilities** for 16 food tree species in Burkina Faso, quantifying climate change risks (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/3BTC8J).
- **ICRAF** established **Sentinel Sites in Kenya** to monitor agroforestry’s role in climate resilience and ecosystem health (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MSGNOU).
agroforestry.adaptation:
• **ICRAF** generated **spatially explicit threat maps** for 16 food tree species in Burkina Faso, assessing climate change and land-use pressures (e.g., overexploitation, fire) to inform resilience strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/3BTC8J).
• **ICRAF** developed **India’s National Agroforestry Policy**, explicitly framing agroforestry as a tool to enhance agricultural resilience to climate risks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/35592).
• **ICRAF** implemented **Sentinel Sites in western Kenya** to track land-use/land-cover changes and agroforestry’s adaptive potential for smallholder farmers (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MSGNOU).
• **CGIAR (via SOIL DATABASE)** supported the **Sustainable Amazonian Landscapes Project** in Colombia/Peru, providing data to strengthen farmers’ adaptive capacity to climate change (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BTRJKD).
agroforestry.mitigation:
• **ICRAF** contributed to **India’s National Agroforestry Policy**, highlighting agroforestry’s dual role in **climate mitigation** (e.g., carbon benefits) and adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/35592).
• **CGIAR** analyzed **agroforestry commitments in UNFCCC NDCs**, categorizing policy-level linkages to carbon sequestration and climate goals (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/KMBUHE).
• **CGIAR (via SOIL DATABASE)** advanced **sustainable land-use models** in the Amazon, aligning agroforestry with **mitigation and environmental service goals** (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BTRJKD).

livestock (dairy).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart and low-emission dairy systems** in **Asia and East Africa** through **farmer-led adaptation strategies, mitigation guidelines, and policy-influenced investments**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** developed **greenhouse gas mitigation guidelines** for dairy farmers in China, focusing on **feed and manure management** (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119591).
- **CGIAR’s International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)** implemented **farmer-led climate adaptation networks** in Kenya, showcasing **pioneer adaptation farmers** as local solutions to climate change in dairy (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/175700).
livestock (dairy).adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** conducted a **socio-economic baseline survey** in East Africa (Tanzania and Rwanda) to assess **Climate-Smart Dairy (CSD) systems**, emphasizing **improved forages and feeding strategies** for resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9GBKPW).
• **CGIAR’s ILRI** supported **50 pioneer adaptation farmers** in Kenya, fostering **farmer-led networks** to share locally developed climate-resilient dairy practices (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/175700).
livestock (dairy).mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** provided **greenhouse gas mitigation guidelines** for dairy farmers in China, covering **feed and manure management** and a **total-farm approach** to reduce emissions (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119591).
• **CGIAR’s research** informed **Kenya’s USD 223 million NAMA (Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Action)** for the dairy sector, leveraging **Green Climate Fund alignment** (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106306).
• **CGIAR shared international best practices** on **low-emission dairy strategies** (e.g., U.S. case studies) to pilot **scaling solutions in China** (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110054).

forages and pastures.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers advanced **forages and pastures** through **germplasm evaluation, agronomic trait selection, and climate-resilient production systems** across Latin America and Ethiopia. Specifically:
- **CIAT** evaluated **adaptation potential of tropical pasture germplasm** in semievergreen forests via the RIEPT network (Source A).
- **CIAT/CORPOICA** identified **superior Brachiaria accessions** with climate-adaptive traits through multilocational trials (Source B).
- **CIAT** analyzed **genotype-environment interactions** in *Desmodium ovalifolium* to mitigate tannin variability under varying soil/climate conditions (Source C).
- **ICARDA/ILRI** assessed **land suitability and supplemental irrigation needs** for alfalfa under climate change in Ethiopia (Source D).
forages and pastures.adaptation:
• **CIAT** established the **RIEPT network** to evaluate pasture germplasm adaptation across tropical ecosystems, comparing productivity, persistence, and management efficiency under grazing systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZB4DYK).
• **CIAT/CORPOICA** selected **Brachiaria accessions** with superior agronomic traits, testing climate-soil interactions on yield across 11 Colombian sites (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NGL4OG).
• **CIAT** conducted **multilocational trials** on *Desmodium ovalifolium* to quantify soil fertility and climate effects on tannin content, addressing anti-quality factors (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/COQEK8).
forages and pastures.water: • **ICARDA/ILRI** developed a **simplified land-suitability framework** for alfalfa production in Ethiopia, integrating supplemental irrigation needs and climate change impact assessments (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123185).

dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient dryland cereal systems** in **West and South Asia** through **adaptive fertility management and weather-resilient technologies**. Specifically:
- CGIAR researchers evaluated **integrated organic-inorganic fertility scenarios** to enhance sorghum productivity under climate model projections in Mali’s Sudanian region (Source A).
- CGIAR promoted **weather-resilient cultivation practices** for pearl millet (Bajra) in Uttar Pradesh, India, addressing lodging risks from excess rainfall and poor irrigation (Source B).
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).adaptation:
• CGIAR researchers **assessed marginal cost-benefit of fertility adaptation options** (organic/inorganic combinations) for sorghum using CMIP5 climate projections to improve productivity and resilience in Mali’s Sudanian region (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SGHJ3J).
• CGIAR **developed and promoted weather-resilient technologies** for pearl millet (Bajra) in Mathura, India, mitigating lodging risks from excess rainfall and scanty irrigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109724).
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).water: • CGIAR **integrated soil moisture availability data** into crop simulation models to evaluate sorghum productivity under climate change, focusing on water-limited agro-ecologies in Mali (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SGHJ3J).

fruits and vegetables.narrative:
CGIAR supported **climate-resilient agricultural systems** in **Burkina Faso** through **water access and crop diversification**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR facilitated microfinance-enabled water infrastructure** (e.g., small pumps) to enhance vegetable production during dry seasons (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061).
- **CGIAR promoted crop diversification** (e.g., vegetables) to stabilize food security and income during off-harvest periods (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061).
fruits and vegetables.adaptation: • **CGIAR enabled farmer-led crop diversification** (e.g., vegetables) to extend productivity and income during dry seasons, reducing famine risks in Burkina Faso (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061).
fruits and vegetables.water: • **CGIAR supported small-scale water infrastructure** (e.g., microfinance-backed pumps) to access dam water for vegetable gardening, improving dry-season agricultural activity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061).

livestock (small).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **small livestock mitigation** in **Ethiopia** through **enteric methane emission quantification**. Specifically:
- The Center developed **Tier 1 and Tier 2 methodologies** to estimate intake and enteric methane emission factors for smallholder sheep systems, integrating live weight measurements, feed quality data, and seasonal digestibility assessments (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4K20I6).
livestock (small).mitigation: • CGIAR **compared Tier 1 and Tier 2 methodologies** to estimate **enteric methane emission factors** and **daily methane production (DMP)** for smallholder sheep in Ethiopia, deriving annual emission factors via seasonal weighted averages (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4K20I6).
